topic: The Early Church: The Church in Acts
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Who do Sikhs consider their living guru today?
items.correct: The Guru Granth Sahib — the Sikh scripture, declared the eternal guru by Guru Gobind Singh after the line of ten human gurus ended.
items.misconception: Sikhs follow a current living guru as their leader.
items.random_wrong: Sikhs consider the Dalai Lama their current guru.
items.why: The line of human gurus ended in 1708; guruship passed to scripture. Students assume a continuing line of personal teachers — missing the distinctive Sikh teaching about the eternal guru in the text.

items.q: What is the central principle of situation ethics?
items.correct: The only intrinsically good thing is agape love; rules can be broken if doing so produces the most loving outcome in the situation.
items.misconception: You should do whatever feels right in the moment.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics says rules should always be followed.
items.why: Situation ethics is principled (love), not just intuition or relativism. Students collapse it into 'whatever feels right' — Fletcher's actual theory is more disciplined than that.

items.q: What is the relationship between atman and Brahman in Hindu philosophy?
items.correct: Atman is the individual self/soul; Brahman is ultimate reality. In Advaita Vedanta, atman IS Brahman — the self is identical with ultimate reality, and liberation comes from realising this non-duality.
items.misconception: Atman is the body and Brahman is the soul.
items.random_wrong: Atman and Brahman are two competing Hindu gods.
items.why: The atman-Brahman identity is the central insight of Advaita. Students invert the soul/body labels or treat both as deities. The teaching is metaphysical: separation between self and ultimate reality is illusion (maya).
It's Aquinas. “Anselm” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Hume. “Descartes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many of Aristotle's causes are there?
items.why: The real figure is 4 causes. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
